content.text: What a Model Card Is

A model card is a short document published alongside an ML model that describes what the model does, how it was trained, what it is intended to be used for, where it fails, and what ethical considerations apply. Introduced by Mitchell et al. (2019) at Google, model cards have since become an industry standard and are increasingly referenced in regulation — the EU AI Act Article 11 technical documentation requirements align closely with a comprehensive model card.
Model cards serve three audiences with different needs:
Downstream developers
integrating the model into products need to know capabilities, limitations, and acceptable use.
Affected communities
need transparency about how the model was trained and where it may be biased.
Regulators and auditors
need structured evidence of due diligence, risk assessment, and compliance.

attrs.alt: Model card anatomy: 8 sections across 2 rows — Model Details, Intended Use, Training Data, Performance (row 1); Risks & Limitations, Ethical Considerations, Human Oversight, Regulatory (row 2)

content.text: Authoring a Model Card: Complete Schema

The following dataclass captures all fields of a production-ready model card. Every field either satisfies a specific regulatory requirement or addresses a transparency need identified in the model card literature:

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Optional

@dataclass
class PerformanceMetric:
    metric_name: str       # e.g. "BLEU", "F1", "accuracy", "toxicity_rate"
    value: float
    dataset: str           # which eval dataset
    subgroup: Optional[str] = None   # e.g. "gender:female", "language:es"
    confidence_interval: Optional[tuple[float, float]] = None

@dataclass
class RiskEntry:
    risk_id: str
    description: str
    severity: str          # "low" | "medium" | "high" | "critical"
    likelihood: str        # "rare" | "possible" | "likely"
    mitigation: str
    residual_risk: str

@dataclass
class ModelCard:
    # ── Model Details ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    model_name: str
    model_version: str
    model_type: str        # "foundation" | "fine_tuned" | "adapter" | "embedding"
    architecture: str      # e.g. "transformer decoder, 7B parameters"
    release_date: str      # ISO-8601
    developer: str
    contact_email: str
    license: str           # SPDX identifier or URL
    model_url: str
    paper_citation: str    # bibtex or DOI

    # ── Intended Use ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    intended_uses: list[str]
    out_of_scope_uses: list[str]
    primary_users: list[str]
    required_expertise: str
    deployment_context: str

    # ── Training Data ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    training_data_summary: str
    training_data_sources: list[dict]  # [{name, size, date_range, license}]
    training_cutoff_date: str
    preprocessing_steps: list[str]
    data_limitations: list[str]

    # ── Performance ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    evaluation_approach: str
    metrics: list[PerformanceMetric]
    known_failure_modes: list[str]
    performance_notes: str

    # ── Risks & Limitations ──────────────────────────────────────────────────
    risks: list[RiskEntry]
    bias_evaluation: dict[str, str]   # dimension -> finding (e.g. "gender" -> "slight over-refusal for female names")
    adversarial_robustness: str
    uncertainty_handling: str

    # ── Ethical Considerations ───────────────────────────────────────────────
    fairness_analysis: str
    privacy_implications: list[str]
    potential_misuse: list[str]
    dual_use_concerns: str
    consent_and_rights: str

    # ── Human Oversight ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    oversight_level_required: str  # "none" | "spot_check" | "human_in_loop" | "human_decision"
    override_mechanism: str
    audit_logging: str
    escalation_path: str

    # ── Regulatory ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    eu_ai_act_tier: str            # "unacceptable" | "high" | "limited" | "minimal"
    nist_ai_rmf_tier: str
    applicable_regulations: list[str]
    dpia_completed: bool
    dpia_reference: str
    version_history: list[dict]   # [{version, date, changes}]
Filling in a Model Card: Worked Example
The following is a concrete model card for a hypothetical customer support LLM assistant. It illustrates the level of specificity required to be useful rather than decorative:

EXAMPLE_CARD = ModelCard(
    # ── Model Details ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    model_name="SupportBot-v2",
    model_version="2.1.0",
    model_type="fine_tuned",
    architecture="Fine-tune of a 7B-parameter transformer decoder on 4,200 support tickets",
    release_date="2025-03-15",
    developer="AcmeCorp AI Team",
    contact_email="ai-safety@acmecorp.example",
    license="Proprietary — internal use only",
    model_url="https://internal.acmecorp.example/models/supportbot-v2",
    paper_citation="",

    # ── Intended Use ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    intended_uses=[
        "First-line triage and response for customer support tickets",
        "Drafting replies for human agent review",
        "Summarising long ticket threads for agent context",
    ],
    out_of_scope_uses=[
        "Final autonomous responses to high-value or legal claims (must go to human)",
        "Medical, legal, or financial advice",
        "Any use case outside AcmeCorp product support domain",
    ],
    primary_users=["AcmeCorp customer support agents", "Support operations tooling"],
    required_expertise="No ML expertise required; UI surfaces model output to agents",
    deployment_context="Internal support platform; agents review and approve all responses before sending",

    # ── Training Data ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    training_data_summary="4,200 anonymised customer support tickets from Jan 2023–Dec 2024, covering billing, product usage, and account issues.",
    training_data_sources=[
        {"name": "AcmeCorp support tickets", "size": "4,200 examples",
         "date_range": "2023-01-01 to 2024-12-31", "license": "internal"},
    ],
    training_cutoff_date="2024-12-31",
    preprocessing_steps=[
        "HIPAA Safe Harbor de-identification applied",
        "PII scan with python-regex + spaCy NER; manual review of flagged items",
        "Removed tickets containing third-party company names",
        "Human quality review of 10% random sample",
    ],
    data_limitations=[
        "Only English-language tickets; multilingual performance untested",
        "Skewed toward billing issues (62% of training data)",
        "No tickets from mobile-app-specific issues prior to Feb 2024",
    ],

    # ── Performance ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    evaluation_approach="Hold-out set of 420 tickets (10%); human rater panel of 5 agents for qualitative scores",
    metrics=[
        PerformanceMetric("ROUGE-L", 0.61, "hold-out-420"),
        PerformanceMetric("agent_approval_rate", 0.87, "hold-out-420"),
        PerformanceMetric("first_contact_resolution_lift", 0.12, "A/B test, n=1200"),
        PerformanceMetric("agent_approval_rate", 0.79, "hold-out-420", subgroup="billing_dispute"),
        PerformanceMetric("agent_approval_rate", 0.91, "hold-out-420", subgroup="product_how_to"),
    ],
    known_failure_modes=[
        "Hallucinates specific policy dates not in training data (seen in 3.2% of billing queries)",
        "Confuses similar product SKUs introduced after training cutoff",
        "Overly formal tone for casual user messages",
    ],
    performance_notes="Approval rate drops to 0.79 for billing disputes — highest-stakes category; recommend mandatory human review for all billing disputes.",

    # ── Risks & Limitations ──────────────────────────────────────────────────
    risks=[
        RiskEntry("R-01", "Model hallucinates refund policy amounts",
                  "high", "possible",
                  "Agent review required; policy lookup tool added to grounding context",
                  "low"),
        RiskEntry("R-02", "PII leakage from training data memorisation",
                  "high", "rare",
                  "PII redaction in training data; output PII scanner applied",
                  "low"),
        RiskEntry("R-03", "Biased responses for non-English speakers who write in broken English",
                  "medium", "possible",
                  "Agent sensitivity training; plan to add multilingual fine-tune in v3",
                  "medium"),
    ],
    bias_evaluation={
        "gender": "No statistically significant difference in approval rates by inferred gender (p=0.43)",
        "language_quality": "7.2% lower approval rate for messages with heavy spelling errors — known gap",
        "ticket_age": "Older tickets (>90 days) have 4.1% lower ROUGE-L — stale context issue",
    },
    adversarial_robustness="Not evaluated against adversarial attacks; internal tool only, not exposed to untrusted users",
    uncertainty_handling="Model does not express calibrated uncertainty; agents are trained to flag low-confidence drafts",

    # ── Ethical Considerations ───────────────────────────────────────────────
    fairness_analysis="Disaggregated evaluation completed across gender and language-quality subgroups. Language-quality gap documented and tracked as known issue R-03.",
    privacy_implications=[
        "Training data de-identified per HIPAA Safe Harbor",
        "Inference inputs (ticket content) not retained beyond 30 days",
        "No customer data sent to third-party LLM providers",
    ],
    potential_misuse=[
        "Agents could use model to mass-deny legitimate claims — mitigated by approval logging and QA sampling",
    ],
    dual_use_concerns="Low — internal tool only; not externally accessible",
    consent_and_rights="Customers notified in privacy policy that support interactions may be processed by automated tools",

    # ── Human Oversight ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    oversight_level_required="human_in_loop",
    override_mechanism="Agents can edit, reject, or replace any model draft before sending",
    audit_logging="Every model output, agent edit, approval, and rejection logged with agent ID and timestamp; retained 365 days",
    escalation_path="Billing disputes → senior agent. Legal threats → legal team. Any hallucination report → AI safety team within 24h",

    # ── Regulatory ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    eu_ai_act_tier="limited",   # AI assistant; Art. 52 disclosure required
    nist_ai_rmf_tier="medium",
    applicable_regulations=["EU AI Act Art. 52", "GDPR", "CCPA"],
    dpia_completed=True,
    dpia_reference="DPIA-2025-03-supportbot",
    version_history=[
        {"version": "1.0.0", "date": "2024-06-01", "changes": "Initial release"},
        {"version": "2.0.0", "date": "2025-01-15", "changes": "Retrained with 2024 data; added grounding tool"},
        {"version": "2.1.0", "date": "2025-03-15", "changes": "Fixed billing hallucination (R-01); improved SKU disambiguation"},
    ],
)
Generating Markdown from a Model Card

Model cards are often published as Markdown documents in a model repository or as part of a Hugging Face model hub page. Automating card generation from the structured dataclass ensures consistency and prevents fields from going stale:

def render_model_card_md(card: ModelCard) -> str:
    """Render a ModelCard to Markdown."""
    lines = [
        f"# Model Card: {card.model_name} {card.model_version}",
        "",
        "## Model Details",
        f"| Field | Value |",
        f"|-------|-------|",
        f"| Type | {card.model_type} |",
        f"| Architecture | {card.architecture} |",
        f"| Released | {card.release_date} |",
        f"| Developer | {card.developer} |",
        f"| License | {card.license} |",
        "",
        "## Intended Use",
        "**Primary uses:**",
        *[f"- {u}" for u in card.intended_uses],
        "",
        "**Out of scope:**",
        *[f"- {u}" for u in card.out_of_scope_uses],
        "",
        "## Training Data",
        card.training_data_summary,
        "",
        "**Known data limitations:**",
        *[f"- {l}" for l in card.data_limitations],
        "",
        "## Performance",
        "| Metric | Value | Dataset | Subgroup |",
        "|--------|-------|---------|----------|",
        *[f"| {m.metric_name} | {m.value:.3f} | {m.dataset} | {m.subgroup or '—'} |"
          for m in card.metrics],
        "",
        "**Known failure modes:**",
        *[f"- {f}" for f in card.known_failure_modes],
        "",
        "## Risks and Limitations",
        "| ID | Description | Severity | Likelihood | Residual Risk |",
        "|----|-------------|----------|------------|---------------|",
        *[f"| {r.risk_id} | {r.description} | {r.severity} | {r.likelihood} | {r.residual_risk} |"
          for r in card.risks],
        "",
        "## Bias Evaluation",
        *[f"- **{dim}**: {finding}" for dim, finding in card.bias_evaluation.items()],
        "",
        "## Ethical Considerations",
        card.fairness_analysis,
        "",
        "**Privacy implications:**",
        *[f"- {p}" for p in card.privacy_implications],
        "",
        "## Human Oversight",
        f"**Required oversight level:** {card.oversight_level_required}",
        f"**Override mechanism:** {card.override_mechanism}",
        f"**Audit logging:** {card.audit_logging}",
        "",
        "## Regulatory",
        f"- EU AI Act tier: {card.eu_ai_act_tier}",
        f"- NIST AI RMF: {card.nist_ai_rmf_tier}",
        f"- DPIA completed: {'Yes' if card.dpia_completed else 'No'} ({card.dpia_reference})",
        f"- Applicable regulations: {', '.join(card.applicable_regulations)}",
        "",
        "## Version History",
        "| Version | Date | Changes |",
        "|---------|------|---------|",
        *[f"| {v['version']} | {v['date']} | {v['changes']} |" for v in card.version_history],
    ]
    return "\n".join(lines)
Model Card Anti-Patterns
Model cards fail their purpose when they treat documentation as a box to check rather than a communication tool. Common anti-patterns to avoid:
Anti-pattern
Problem
Fix
Generic performance numbers
"Accuracy: 0.92" with no dataset, subgroup, or date context
Report metric + dataset + subgroup + date + confidence interval
Empty or placeholder risks
"May produce inaccurate results" — tells the reader nothing
Describe specific failure modes with reproduction rate if known
Copy-pasted bias section
Identical bias text across every model in an organisation
Run actual disaggregated evaluation; report findings, not process
Version not linked to model
Card describes v1 but is attached to v2 artifact
Pin card version to model artifact hash in CI/CD pipeline
Out-of-scope list too vague
"Do not use for harmful purposes"
List concrete prohibited use cases your actual users might attempt
No update process
Card is published once and never updated as the model is fine-tuned or issues are discovered
Version history section + update trigger in model deployment pipeline
Integrating Model Cards into CI/CD
A model card that is not updated when the model changes is worse than no card — it creates false confidence. Enforce card currency in your deployment pipeline:

import hashlib, json
from pathlib import Path

def validate_model_card(card_path: Path, model_artifact_path: Path) -> list[str]:
    """
    Run pre-deployment model card validation.
    Returns list of errors; empty list = card is valid.
    """
    errors = []

    with open(card_path) as f:
        card_data = json.load(f)

    # 1. Card must reference the current model version
    model_hash = hashlib.sha256(model_artifact_path.read_bytes()).hexdigest()[:16]
    if card_data.get("model_artifact_hash") != model_hash:
        errors.append(
            f"Card model_artifact_hash {card_data.get('model_artifact_hash')!r} "
            f"does not match artifact hash {model_hash!r}. Update the card."
        )

    # 2. Required sections must be non-empty
    required_fields = [
        "model_name", "model_version", "intended_uses", "out_of_scope_uses",
        "risks", "bias_evaluation", "eu_ai_act_tier",
    ]
    for field in required_fields:
        value = card_data.get(field)
        if not value:
            errors.append(f"Required field '{field}' is empty or missing")

    # 3. Performance metrics must include at least one disaggregated result
    metrics = card_data.get("metrics", [])
    has_subgroup = any(m.get("subgroup") for m in metrics)
    if not has_subgroup:
        errors.append("No disaggregated performance metric found; add at least one subgroup result")

    # 4. EU AI Act tier must be set and valid
    valid_tiers = {"unacceptable", "high", "limited", "minimal"}
    tier = card_data.get("eu_ai_act_tier", "")
    if tier not in valid_tiers:
        errors.append(f"eu_ai_act_tier must be one of {valid_tiers}, got {tier!r}")

    return errors


# In your deployment script:
# errors = validate_model_card(Path("model_card.json"), Path("model.bin"))
# if errors:
#     print("\n".join(errors))
#     sys.exit(1)   # block deployment
Summary

Model cards are the primary mechanism for transparent, accountable AI deployment. A good model card covers eight areas: model identity and provenance, intended and prohibited uses, training data sources and limitations, disaggregated performance evaluation, specific risk entries with severity and mitigation, ethical considerations including fairness and privacy, human oversight requirements, and regulatory classification. The card must be version-controlled and linked to the exact model artifact it describes — a card that goes stale with each model update creates false assurance. Automating card rendering from a structured schema and enforcing card validity as a CI/CD gate are the practices that make model cards operationally effective rather than ceremonially compliant.
Key principle:

The measure of a good model card is whether a downstream developer who has never spoken to the model authors can make an informed decision about whether and how to deploy it. If the card does not enable that decision, it is not yet good enough.
